coredata.dc:title: Neutral red derived red emitting carbon dots and multifunctional applications: pH sensing, anti-counterfeiting and latent fingerprints imaging

Yang, Shaowen
Guo, Xiangjun
Fu, Liming
Jian, Ke
Zhao, Xihui
coredata.dc:description: Green and red emission carbon dots (G-CDs and R-CDs) with different luminescence properties were prepared using neutral red and oxalic acid through solvothermal reactions and a simple layering procedure. The obtained R-CDs possess more hydrophilic functional groups than G-CDs, exhibiting better hydrophilicity and more sensitive pH responsiveness. Furthermore, based on their different colors and responsiveness to acidity and alkalinity they can be used for drawing fluorescent anti-counterfeiting patterns. In addition, solid-state fluorescent CDs composite materials (S-G-CDs and S-R-CDs) were obtained by doping starch matrix with G-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. More importantly, S-R-CDs still maintain a significant pH response, which can be further applied in LFPs detection and anti-counterfeiting, expanding the application fields of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emitting carbon dots
PH response
Anti-counterfeiting
Fingerprint detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence determination of pH and preparation of response test strips
Preparation of S-G-CDs and S-R-CDs and latent fingerprint imaging
Synthesis and characterization of G-CDs and R-CDs
Optical properties of G-CDs and R-CDs
Optical properties and applications of solid CDs composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A-B) TEM images, HRTEM (top illustration) and particle size distribution (bottom illustration) of G-CDs and R-CDs. (C) XRD of G-CDs and R-CDs. (D) FT-IR of G-CDs, R-CDs and neutral red. (E-F) XPS survey spectrum of G-CDs and R-CDs. (G-I) High-resolution XPS of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively.

(A-B) UV–vis absorption, optimal excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs. Inset: photographs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light. (C) The CIE chromaticity coordinates of G-CDs and R-CDs. (D-E) Fluorescence emission spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (F) Fluorescence decay curves of G-CDs and R-CDs.

(A, B) Photographs of R-CDs at different pH values under sunlight and 365 nm UV light. (C) UV absorption spectra of R-CDs at different pH values. (D-E) Fluorescence emission spectra of R-CDs with different pH values at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. (F) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH values. (G) Reversible fluorescence response with pH values between 3.0 and 11.0. (H) Fluorescence response photographs of R-CDs test strips at different pH.

(A-C) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs under different excitations at pH values of 3, 7, and 11. (D-F) The CIE chromaticity coordinates, photographs under 365 nm light and zeta potential of R-CDs at pH values of 3, 7, and 11 (G) Response mechanism of R-CDs for pH sensing.

(A) Anti-counterfeiting patterns of G-CDs fluorescent ink under sunlight and UV light. (B) A morning glory anti-counterfeiting pattern designed with G-CDs and R-CDs fluorescent ink. (C) Fluorescence epoxy resin model of G-CDs (up) and R-CDs (down).

(A) The optimal excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of S-G-CDs. Inset: photographs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of S-G-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (C) Contour plots of S-G-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (D) The optimal excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of S-R-CDs. (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of S-R-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (F) Contour plots of S-R-CDs. (G) Photographs of sunflower clusters drawn in a solid acid-base state under sunlight and 365 nm UV light. (H) Photographs of S-R-CDs in an acid-base state on a glass plate, magnified images: (1) core, (2) termination, (3) scar, (4) bifurcate.

head.ce:title: Neutral red derived red emitting carbon dots and multifunctional applications: pH sensing, anti-counterfeiting and latent fingerprints imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bio-fibers and Eco-textiles, Shandong Collaborative Innovation Center of Marine Biobased Fibers and Ecological textiles, Institute of Marine Biobased Materials, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qingdao University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green and red emission carbon dots (G-CDs and R-CDs) with different luminescence properties were prepared using neutral red and oxalic acid through solvothermal reactions and a simple layering procedure. The obtained R-CDs possess more hydrophilic functional groups than G-CDs, exhibiting better hydrophilicity and more sensitive pH responsiveness. Furthermore, based on their different colors and responsiveness to acidity and alkalinity they can be used for drawing fluorescent anti-counterfeiting patterns. In addition, solid-state fluorescent CDs composite materials (S-G-CDs and S-R-CDs) were obtained by doping starch matrix with G-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. More importantly, S-R-CDs still maintain a significant pH response, which can be further applied in LFPs detection and anti-counterfeiting, expanding the application fields of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widespread attention as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial due to their simple preparation methods, excellent optical properties and high photostability. Therefore, CDs are widely used in many fields, such as sensing, anti-counterfeiting, optoelectronics, and catalysis [1–3]. Unfortunately, most CDs display blue-to-green photoluminescence and long wavelength emission CDs are difficult to obtain [4,5]. Therefore, efficient long wavelength emission CDs has become a research hotspot. As is well known, long wavelength emission CDs usually possess a large π−π* conjugation degree in their core [6]. Therefore, derivatives of benzene, naphthalene, etc. can be selected as carbon sources, which can react to construct larger cyclic structures [7,8]. Yuan et al. hydrothermally prepared full-color luminescent CDs with 1,8-diaminonaphthalene and citric acid in concentrated sulfuric acid. The highest fluorescence emission wavelength reaches 604 nm. The authors believe that 1,8-diaminonaphthalene can be regarded as a rigid conjugated carbon skeleton with amino groups, which can be directly carbonized into a sp2 carbon core with appropriate size and few defects [9]. This is the main reason for the red emission of CDs. In addition, regulating the surface state of CDs is also one of the important ways to achieve long-wavelength emission. The functional groups such as carboxyl, amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs will introduce defects at the edge of the conjugated structure of CDs and introduce new energy levels in the band gap, thus facilitating the long-wavelength emission [10]. It is worth noting that dye molecules, as a special kind of aromatic small molecule with functional groups, are often used as precursors for preparing long-wavelength CDs. During the synthesis process, the inherent conjugated electronic structure of dye molecules may be partially retained. This is beneficial for preparing red-emitting carbon dots that have similar excitation and emission properties to dye precursor molecules, while exhibiting greatly enhanced stability and being more environmentally friendly. Significantly, neutral red, as a commonly used phenazine dye, not only contains multiple aromatic rings and double bonds, making it an excellent candidate for preparing long wavelength emitting carbon dots, but also its optical properties are highly sensitive to the surrounding environment, especially to pH [11]. During the reaction process, neutral red will partially carbonize, which contributes to form the carbon core; meanwhile, the uncarbonized precursor portion will be incorporated into the structure of the CDs, thus taking responsibility for the final optical properties of the resulting CDs, even its sensitivity to the surrounding environment, which facilitates sensing application of CDs [6,12]. Mu et al. synthesized red fluorescent CDs using neutral red and tartaric acid through a simple hydrothermal method, and the obtained CDs can be used to precisely monitor moisture content in DMF [13]. Chang et al. synthesized red fluorescent CDs with neutral red and malic acid for detecting potassium permanganate in biological systems [14]. Meng et al. synthesized orange fluorescent CDs using neutral red and polyethyleneimine for the detection of glutathione [15]. However, despite that some success has been achieved in synthesizing CDs with neutral red, challenges still remain in the preparation of highly fluorescent CDs with long-wavelength emission, particularly in terms of pH sensing.

Additionally, although most CDs have good water solubility, their fluorescence disappears in the solid state owing to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect, this greatly affects their practical applications, especially in high-sensitivity latent fingerprint (LFPs) imaging based on CDs [3,16]. Besides, this is also attributed to the fact that powdered CDs are easier to detect fingerprints on the substrate than liquid CDs. Therefore, researchers usually use some matrices to disperse CDs, so that CDs not only retain their fluorescence performance but also achieve better fluidity [2,17]. However, most CDs used for LFPs exhibit shortwave emission, while CDs with longwave emission are still rare and more suitable as fluorescent fingerprint markers.

Herein, Green and red emission carbon dots (G-CDs and R-CDs) were prepared using neutral red and oxalic acid only by solvothermal reaction and a simple layering procedure ( Scheme 1). Interestingly, R-CDs retained the main structure of neutral red and exhibited a significant response to pH similar to neutral red. Not only is there a significant difference in fluorescence color and intensity, but during the process from acid to alkali, the color under sunlight changes from red to yellow. Consequently, these optical properties are conducive to pH detection. Noticeably, although G-CDs are less sensitive to pH than R-CDs, showing different color compared to R-CDs at different pH value. And based on this, the designed fluorescent ink patterns can also be used for anti-counterfeiting. In addition, S-R-CDs and S-G-CDs were prepared by doping starch matrix, obtaining solid-state fluorescence CDs composites. More importantly, S-R-CDs still maintain a significant pH response, which can be further applied in LFPs detection and anti-counterfeiting, expanding the application fields of CDs.
Detailed information on reagents and instruments was provided in Supplementary Material.

G-CDs and R-CDs were synthesized with neutral red and oxalic acid by the hydrothermal method. Briefly, neutral red (0.002 g) and oxalic acid (0.1 g) were dissolved in 10 ml water and ultrasonic treatment for 10 min until completely dissolved. Then transferred the uniform solution to a 20 ml high-pressure vessel and reacted at 200 ℃ for 4 h. After the reaction, a purple red stock solution was obtained. Then, the stock solution was sonicated for 20 min, followed by the addition of 10 ml n-hexane and 5 ml ethanol in sequence. After standing and layering, green and red fluorescence CDs solutions, namely G-CDs (11 ml, 0.23 mg/ml) and R-CDs (14 ml, 0.67 mg/ml) were obtained and stored at 4 °C for further experiments. As a comparison, CDs were prepared using only neutral red with the same method, named C-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of pH and preparation of response test strips
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, R-CDs were diluted with deionized water, and then adjusted with NaOH or HCl solution to obtain a series of CDs solutions with different pH. Fluorescence spectra of CDs solutions with different pH values were recorded with excitation wavelength 360 nm. Slit was 5 nm.

Evenly dropped 3 ml of R-CDs onto filter paper, then dried to obtain fluorescence filter paper containing CDs. Then the fluorescence filter paper was evenly divided into 1 cm × 3 cm strips for further testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of S-G-CDs and S-R-CDs and latent fingerprint imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Measured 3 ml of G-CDs and R-CDs into a 10 ml beaker. Then added 0.02 g of starch separately, stirred well and sonicated for 15 min until the mixture was uniform. Finally, transferred the mixture to a 60 °C oven and dried for 4 h. After grinding, white and rose red powders were obtained, named S-G-CDs and S-R-CDs.

The fingerprint comes from a male's right index finger. Firstly, cleaned your fingerprints with soap, gently wiped your right index finger on your forehead, and imprinted it on a glass plate. Then, sprayed the powder onto the glass plate and used a brush to remove excess powder. Finally, under sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet light, the marked fingerprints were very clear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of G-CDs and R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal method. As is well known, neutral red belongs to phenazine dyes and has a fluorescent emission center and a large molecular structure [18]. In addition, during the synthesis process, the reaction temperature was set at 200 °C, which is lower than the decomposition temperature of neutral red. This not only facilitates carbonization under high temperature conditions, but also preserves the partial original structure of neutral red [7]. In the hydrothermal environment of high temperature and high pressure, neutral red molecules undergo a series of chemical reactions such as polymerization and carbonization. During the reaction process, the molecular structure of neutral red gradually changes to form a carbon core and various functional groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups are formed on the surface, finally forming carbon dots [6]. In this process of preparing CDs, oxalic acid may play important roles: it can provide an acidic environment, which promotes the protonation of neutral red molecules, causing some chemical bonds in neutral red molecules to break or form more easily, thus triggering a polymerization reaction and then forming the precursors of CDs. Additionally, at a relatively high temperature, oxalic acid may undergo decomposition or dehydration reactions to produce some carbon-containing intermediate products. These carbon-containing intermediate products can interact with neutral red molecules or their reaction products to promote the carbonization reaction and accelerate the formation of CDs. Moreover, it may participate in the formation of surface functional groups of CDs, such as hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH), or adjust the chemical composition of the CDs surface, thus changing the fluorescence emission wavelength and increasing the fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1). The original solution obtained after the reaction was purified and separation by adding n-Hexane and ethanol to obtain G-CDs and R-CDs (Scheme 1).

The structure and morphology of the obtained CDs were investigated by TEM, XRD, and FT-IR. The TEM images of G-CDs and R-CDs were shown in Fig. 1 A& B, CDs are both spherical and uniformly dispersed, and the lattice fringes in the HR-TEM images are clearly visible, both at 0.21 nm, corresponding to the 100 crystal planes of graphite[19,20]. From the histogram of particle size distribution in the inset, it can be seen that the average particle sizes of G-CDs and R-CDs are 2.23 nm and 3.71 nm, respectively. AFM measurements further confirmed the particle size and morphology of G-CDs and R-CDs (Fig. S2). From the XRD images of G-CDs and R-CDs (Fig. 1C), it can be seen that there is a broad peak near 26°, which is attributed to the plane of graphite (002) [21,22]. The FT-IR spectra of neutral red, G-CDs, and R-CDs are shown in Fig. 1D. R-CDs (red line) and neutral red (black line) have similar structures, with absorption peaks at 3420 cm−1 and 2930 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of O-H and C-H, respectively[23,24]. In addition, there are functional groups such as CO (1660 cm−1), C-O (1010 cm−1), and C-N (1277 cm−1)[6,25]. Notably, G-CDs (green line) contain fewer surface functional groups such as C-O, and C-N compared to R-CDs. The increase of surface functional groups (as chromophores) can enhance the conjugation effect, leading to a decrease energy level[26]. Besides, the surface compositions of G-CDs and R-CDs were further explored through XPS. The full XPS spectrum of G-CDs (Fig. 1E) shows strong signals of C 1 s (284.82 eV) and O 1 s (532.13 eV), as well as weak signals of N 1 s (398.74 eV), corresponding to contents of 64.05 %, 35.65 %, and 0.30 %, respectively [27,28]. Comparatively, XPS spectrum of R-CDs (Fig. 1F) exhibits the same elemental composition as G-CDs, with corresponding contents of 67.05 %, 30.82 %, and 2.13 % for C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s, respectively. Specifically, in Fig. 1G and Table S1, the C1s fine spectrum of G-CDs is fitted into three parts at 284.48 eV (C-C/CC), 285.04 eV (C-N/C-O), and 287.44 eV (CO), with corresponding contents of 43.36 %, 28.59 %, and 28.05 %, respectively[29]. Correspondingly, the peak centers of R-CDs were 284.28 eV, 284.91 eV, and 287.06 eV, with contents of 20.65 %, 23.79 %, and 55.56 %, respectively [30]. The O 1 s fine spectrum of G-CDs in Fig. 1H displays two peaks at 531.87 eV and 533.94 eV, belonging to CO and C-O, with contents of 36.74 % and 63.26 % respectively [31]. Comparatively, the O 1 s spectrum of R-CDs possesses two peaks ascribed to CO and C-O at 532.03 eV and 533.63 eV, with their relative content 46.35 % and 53.65 %, respectively [32]. The N 1 s of G-CDs in Fig. 1I shows two peaks of 398.55 eV and 399.60 eV, belonging to pyridine N and amino N, with contents of 49.32 % and 50.68 %, respectively. The peaks of R-CDs are at 398.34 eV and 399.36 eV, with corresponding contents of 23.74 % and 76.26 % [12,33]. All these results of FT-IR and XPS demonstrate that R-CDs contain more functional groups and nitrogen content, and their hydrophilicity is superior to G-CDs. The difference between G-CDs and R-CDs will have an impact on their optical performance, leading to different applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of G-CDs and R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the synthesis conditions of CDs were investigated [6]. As shown in Fig. S3, when the precursor is only neutral red and the hydrothermal reaction time is 4 h at 200 ℃, the prepared CDs display the optimal fluorescence emission peak at around 600 nm, and obvious UV–vis spectral characteristic peaks at around 520 nm and 280 nm. While, the optimal emission peak shifts to shorter wavelength, as well as the disappearance of the peak at 600 nm with the increasing time to 8 h and 10 h. At the same time, the UV–vis absorption peak at 520 nm disappears, and the color under sunlight changes from red to light yellow, and the fluorescence color at 365 nm UV light changes from orange yellow to blue. For comparison, using neutral red and oxalic acid as reaction precursors (Fig. S4), the corresponding CDs prepared at 200℃ for 4 h, 8 h, and 10 h exhibit more pronounced dual emission peaks at lower excitation wavelengths, indicating their dual emission center. It is noteworthy that the optimal fluorescence emission wavelengths all present at 600 nm and UV visible spectra all display obvious characteristic peaks at around 520 nm and 280 nm. The results further demonstrate the important role of oxalic acid to during the synthesis process of CDs. Additionally, as controlled experiments, oxalic acid alone undergoes hydrothermal treatment at 200℃ for 4 h, the obtained product (o-CDs) has weak blue fluorescence with emission peak at 430 nm, even functionalize it with neutral red, the fluorescence is also very weak with a redshifted peak at 610 nm (Fig. S5). Furthermore, the effect of reaction temperature was also investigated. The results in Fig. S6 demonstrate that the suitable temperature for obtaining long wavelength emitting CDs is 200℃. Therefore, taking neutral red and oxalic acid as reaction precursors, after hydrothermal carbonization reaction (200℃, 4 h), further purification and separation were carried out by adding n-Hexane and ethanol to obtain two types of CDs. The upper layer appeared light yellow color under sunlight, green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, named G-CDs, which were be dispersed in n-Hexane, and the lower layer appeared red under sunlight with a fluorescent color of rose red, named R-CDs, which were be dispersed in water ( Fig. 2A inset).

The optical properties of G-CDs and R-CDs were further investigated through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of G-CDs shows only one absorption peak at 218 nm, owing to the π-π* transition of CC in the carbon core of G-CDs (Fig. 2A) [34,35]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelengths for G-CDs are located at 410 nm and 498 nm, respectively. While, as shown in Fig. 2B, R-CDs exhibit absorption peaks at 210 nm and 270 nm, due to the π-π* transition of CC [36]. Secondly, there is a broad peak at 520 nm, which is ascribed to the n-π* transitions of CO, C-O, and C-N in the surface structure[37]. The optimal wavelengths of excitation and emission for R-CD display a red shift compared to G-CD. According to reports, CDs with a larger size would emit at longer wavelengths, and the surface chemical state has been proven to be a key factor in regulating the photoluminescence behavior of CDs. R-CDs in present work not only have a larger average size of 3.7 nm than G-CDs (2.2 nm), but also have more surface groups, providing with more surface defects, resulting in narrower band gaps, corresponding to longer excitation/emission wavelengths [38]. In Fig. 2C, the optimal emission colors of G-CDs and R-CDs were further depicted using a chromaticity diagram (CIE), with coordinates of (0.21, 0.38) and (0.35, 0.25), respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence emission spectrum of G-CDs shows a single peak emission (Fig. 2D), and undergoes a red shift with increasing excitation wavelength, showing a clear excitation dependence. As is well known, the fewer surface functional groups make less surface functionalized passivation, resulting in diversity and non-uniformity at the surface edges, and the generation of many new energy levels between n-π* gaps. The red shift of fluorescence spectrum further indicates that G-CDs exhibit non-uniform surface states with different energies at different excitation wavelengths, which can be attributed to less surface functionalization passivation [26]. Unlike G-CDs, R-CDs exhibits bimodal emission, and the emission peak intensity at 600 nm gradually enhances with the increase of excitation wavelength, without significant excitation dependence (Fig. 2E). Noticeably, the fluorescence color is rose red, which is a composite color under excitation at 365 nm ultraviolet light.

The absolute quantum yields (QY) of G-CDs and R-CDs in solution were determined using an integrating sphere, with values of 16.6 % and 13.7 %, respectively. Additionally, the fluorescence decay curve of G-CDs and R-CDs shown in Fig. 2F with average fluorescence lifetimes of 7.03 ns and 1.08 ns, respectively. Notably, the fluorescence decay curves of G-CDs and R-CDs follow single-exponential and bi-exponential decay, respectively, indicating that the luminescence center of G-CDs is mainly derived from carbon core, while R-CDs have two luminescence centers, coming from the π-π* transitions of the carbon core and the complex surface defect state [39,40]. The fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs is very short, indicating that it may have a large number of surface defects. The higher surface functional groups provide R-CDs with more surface defects, resulting in narrower band gaps [38].

Furthermore, the performance of G-CDs and R-CDs in solvents with different polarity was measured (Fig. S7). It can be seen that G-CDs exhibit a slight solvent dependent effect, and the position of the fluorescence emission peak does not change much in different solvents under optimal excitation. Remarkably, G-CDs is insoluble in protonated solvents with strong polarity (H2O, EG). In contrast, the emission peaks of the R-CDs are gradually red-shifted from 566 nm to 605 nm with an increase of the solvent polarity, suggesting the different interaction between solvent and CDs. In proton solvents (EtOH, EG, and H2O), the main interaction is due to the hydrogen bond between the proton solvent and R-CDs, while in nonproton solvents (DMSO and DMF), the change in emission peak position is mainly attributed to the dipole−dipole interaction [41].

It is interesting that the synthesized R-CDs exhibit significant responsiveness to pH value. In Fig. 3 A, as the pH increases from 0.71 to 13.26, it is clear that the color of the CDs solution changes from red (acidic) to orange yellow (neutral), and finally to yellow (alkaline). Under 365 nm UV light, the corresponding fluorescence color of CDs solution changes from dark orange to bright yellow, then to dark yellow (Fig. 3B), meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity changes from weak to strong, then to weak again. These changes of R-CDs at different pH can be distinguished by the naked eye, indicating its potential application in pH sensing[18,42].

Furthermore, the sensing characteristics of R-CDs at different pH values were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 3C). As the pH changes from acidic to neutral and then to alkaline, the absorption peak intensity at 520 nm gradually decreases until it disappears, at the same time, the peak width at 270 nm gradually narrows. This indicates that the functional groups on the surface of CDs are easily protonated and deprotonated, making them more sensitive to pH changes and producing efficient selectivity [43]. Besides, the response of fluorescence emission spectra to pH was also investigated at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. From acidic to neutral and weak alkalinity (Fig. 3D), the emission peaks change from clear bimodal emission located at about 450 nm and 570 nm to single peak emission at 530 nm, meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity is significantly enhanced. However, from weak alkalinity to strong alkalinity, there was no significant change in the emission peak position, while the peak intensity gradually decreased (Fig. 3E). Moreover, the relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH value can be obtained (Fig. 3F). When the pH value is between 0.5 and 7.0, using the exponential growth function in nonlinear fitting, the relationship equation obtained is
Y=- 17259.38 + 20214.08 *
e
0.26 X
(R2=0.9869), where Y refers to the fluorescence intensity and X refers to the corresponding pH value. However, there is a linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH between 7.0 and 11.0, with the formula Y=154694.08–3420.89X (R2=0.8778). At the same time, the relationship remains linear between 11.0 and 13.2, which can be expressed as Y=686806.76–50169.19X (R2=0.9766) [44]. It should be noted that the fluorescence response of R-CDs is reversible when the pH values are 3 and 11 (Fig. 3G). Further research was conducted on its anti-interference ability at pH 3 and 11. As shown in Fig. S8, the relative fluorescence intensity of R-CDs did not show significant changes after adding common interferents such as metal cations, anions and biomolecules. The results indicate that R-CDs possess good specificity for H+ concentration. More interesting, R-CDs can be fabricated into test strips and applied to pH detection, showing different colors to different pH (Fig. 3H). Remarkably, the fluorescence color of R-CDs dispersed in aqueous solution is different from that of the test strips in dry state, which may be due to the different interactions of R-CDs with water molecules and cellulose (test strips). Nevertheless, the test strips fabricated with R-CDs display different colors for solutions with different pH ranges, which is similar to R-CDs aqueous solutions. These results demonstrate that the synthesized R-CDs can serve as efficient fluorescent sensors for detecting pH values.

The fluorescence spectra of R-CDs were measured in detail under different pH conditions. Under acidic conditions (pH=3), with the increasing of the excitation wavelength from 360 nm to 580 nm, the emission peak at 470 nm gradually shifts towards a longer wavelength and weakens, while the position at 600 nm remains almost unchanged and gradually strengthens ( Fig. 4A). Under neutral conditions (pH=7), it presents similar bimodal emission with higher intensity (Fig. 4B). However, under alkaline conditions (pH=11), it exhibits unimodal emission and significant excitation dependence (Fig. 4C). The CIE coordinates of the optimal emission peaks of R-CDs at pH values of 3, 7, and 11 are shown in Fig. 4D, which are (0.34, 0.27), (0.44, 0.49), and (0.41, 0.52), respectively. The corresponding fluorescence color of R-CDs is red at pH=3, and yellow at pH=7 and 11 (Fig. 4E). Significantly, R-CDs possess stronger fluorescence and more pronounced responsiveness to pH than the C-CDs prepared with only neutral red (Fig. S9).

Additionally, the response mechanism of R-CDs to pH were further explored. The most possible mechanism for the pH sensitive property of R-CDs is their reversible protonation/deprotonation under acidic and alkaline conditions [19,45]. On the one hand, at lower pH, the protonation of the CDs is more electrophilic, inducing the strong vibration coupling of –OH and –NH2 functional groups due to H-bond binding, which broadens the energy level and enhances the conformational rigidity of the CDs, thereby affecting the size and fluorescence properties of G-CDs [19]. Simultaneously, –NH2 forms –NH3 + due to protonation, playing an important passivation role [46]. Compared the FT-IR spectrum of the R-CDs at pH = 3 with pH = 11 (Fig. S10), the peak at 3462 cm−1 (–OH/NH2 stretching vibration), 1143 cm−1 (C–OH stretching vibration) increases. Meanwhile, the CO/CN stretching vibration peak at 1636 cm−1 and 1568 cm−1 become weak, while the C–N stretching vibration peak at 1421 cm−1 become strong. All these probably due to the protonation of hydroxyl and amino groups [47]. Moreover, the surface states of R-CDs were validated via the zeta potential (Fig. 4F). At pH=7, the zeta potential value is −2.7 mV, and the surface of R-CDs carries a certain negative charge because of the ionization of a large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface [43]. When the pH value decreased from 7 to 3, the zeta potential value of R-CDs sharply increased to +15 mV, indicating that the hydrophilic groups (-COOH, -OH, and -NH2) on the surface were protonated at low pH values. While, under alkaline conditions (pH=11), the zeta potential value is −10.6, and the negative charge proves deprotonation [44]. Therefore, under neutral and weak alkaline conditions, the deprotonation process enhances the electron donating ability of oxygen and nitrogen, induces intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions, and leads to enhanced fluorescence (Fig. 4G) [17,25]. It must be noted that the structure of the CDs may be damaged at strong acid (pH<3) or strong alkaline (pH>11), further resulting in weak fluorescence.

Significantly, the responsiveness of G-CDs to different pH was also investigated. Due to the hydrophobicity of G-CDs, solid G-CDs were dispersed in ethanol firstly, then adjusted pH to 3, 7 and 11. The corresponding fluorescence spectra of G-CDs display similar excitation dependence at different pH, with no significant change in fluorescence emission peaks but different intensities (Fig.S11).

It is well known that information encryption is one of the important means to ensure information security [1,48]. As shown in Fig. 5A, polar bear was drawn in filter paper using fluorescent ink prepared from G-CDs solution. The pattern does not show obvious colors under sunlight, while appears bright cyan color under 365 nm UV lamp, and the fluorescence brightness and color also have corresponding changes after spraying alkali (pH=11) or acid (pH=3). Furthermore, G-CDs and R-CDs can be applied as security ink together. As shown in Fig. 5B, the morning glory was drawn with G-CD ink (leaf) and R-CD ink (flower). It achieves a multi-level anti-counterfeiting mode under the stimulation of sunlight and UV light, especially in different acid-base environment, creating more possibilities for future anti-counterfeiting.

More importantly, G-CDs and R-CDs can be uniformly dispersed in epoxy resin, which can be further cured by heating, and shaped into different shapes, handicrafts, or decorative materials [17,49], which appear transparent under sunlight, but show blue and orange red fluorescence under UV lights, providing better visual appeal (Fig. 5C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and applications of solid CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: To broaden the application of CDs, solid CDs composites were obtained by mixing the synthesized CDs with starch, named S-G-CDs and S-R-CDs, respectively (Scheme 1). Starch, as a natural polysaccharide rich in hydroxyl groups, is often used as a doping matrix due to its advantages of being non-toxic, inexpensive, and easily obtainable. Moreover, CDs can be adsorbed to starch particles through hydrogen bonding between the surface functional groups of CDs and starch particles, avoiding aggregation induced quenching of CDs and enhancing the luminescence thermal stability [50]. The optimal fluorescence excitation wavelength for S-G-CDs is 420 nm, and the optimal fluorescence emission peak is at 504 nm ( Fig. 6A). The fluorescence color under 365 nm UV light is green, which is the same as the fluorescence color of G-CDs solution. And it is a unimodal emission with obvious excitation dependence (Fig. 6B&C). Correspondingly, for S-R-CDs, the optimal fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths are 500 nm and 625 nm, respectively, and the color is rose red under both sunlight and 365 nm UV light (Fig. 6D). Remarkably, S-R-CDs exhibit significant bimodal emission, with a composite fluorescence color (Fig. 6E&F). Furthermore, the QY of S-G-CDs and S-R-CDs powders were also measured at 17.5 % and 15.8 %, respectively, all higher than those of G-CDs and R-CDs solutions. This may be due to the cross-linking interaction between starch and CDs, which inhibit non-radiative transitions [34]. In addition, the fluorescence spectra of S-G-CDs and S-R-CDs under acidic and alkaline conditions were also measured. The optimal excitation wavelengths of S-G-CDs in both acidic and alkaline states are located at 410 nm, corresponding to an emission wavelength of 470 nm, with slight difference in fluorescence color (Fig. S12A-C). However, the fluorescence spectrum of S-R-CDs exhibits bimodal emission under acidic conditions, with an optimal emission wavelength of 640 nm (Fig. S12D). Under alkaline conditions, it exhibits single peak emission and significant excitation dependence, with an optimal emission wavelength of 500 nm (Fig. S12E). The corresponding colors of S-R-CDs acidic and alkaline solid powders show significant differences (Fig. S12F), appear pink and earthy yellow under sunlight, and red and yellow under ultraviolet light, respectively. More importantly, S-R-CDs not only exhibit sensitive responses to acidic and alkaline conditions, but also exhibit excellent stability to UV irradiation and temperature, even long-term storage (Fig. S13), which are beneficial for practical applications [32,51]. Furthermore, in Fig. 6G, S-R-CDs fill the petals of the sunflower (top), while S-G-CDs fill the stems and leaves of the sunflower (bottom), displaying different color information, confirming the application potential in information encryption.

Moreover, based on the non-toxic and good particle flowability of doped matrix starch, as well as the optical properties of CDs composite materials, they can be applied in fingerprint recognition [49]. It should be pointed out that under acidic and alkaline conditions, the fluorescence colors of S-R-CDs fingerprints are red and yellow, respectively, and are clearly visible (Fig. 6H). The fingerprint ridges attached to the glass plate are clear, and the enlarged area map also shows the feature points of core, termination, scar and bifurcate [50]. These feature points can be identified by analyzing and comparing human fingerprint images. Based on this, the prepared solid CDs composites can be applied to LFPs recognition.

CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using neutral red and oxalic acid as precursors. After further purification, G-CDs and R-CDs with different particle sizes and surface functional groups were obtained. Surprisingly, the synthesized R-CDs exhibit sensitive response to pH with significant color changes. Under acidic conditions, the aggregation of R-CDs induced by hydrogen ions leads to fluorescence quenching, while under alkaline conditions, the deprotonation of hydrophilic groups on the surface induces intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions, thereby enhancing fluorescence. In addition, S-G-CDs and S-R-CDs were obtained by mixing with starch, and they were combined for the drawing of anti-counterfeiting patterns. The acid-base state of S-R-CDs can also be used for LFPs detection. This shows that the synthesized CDs have great application prospects in sensing, anti-counterfeiting, LFPs and other fields.

Zhibin Zhao: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiangjun Guo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ke Jian: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Liming Fu: Software, Methodology, Data curation. Xihui Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation. Shaowen Yang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2022ME159).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2024.177380.
